This is Jennifer in Tennessee. I have been listening to a lot of your talks about hearing the voice of God, and that makes total sense to me. The question that I have is, do you think people are confusing what they're saying about hearing the voice of God maybe with thoughts of being led by the Holy Spirit? What exactly does it mean to be led by the Holy Spirit? Is that a totally different thing than hearing the voice of God in people's minds? Thank you. You're welcome, Jen, in Tennessee. There is a kinship between these two ideas in practice. And what I mean by that is the way people are practicing this concept, which I have weighed in heavily that this is not a biblical, a sound biblical notion. And so the hearing the voice of God in their minds, I'm just talking about the way people talk about it, they mean it in different ways. The point is that God is communicating something substantive that in principle could be expressed propositionally. Now, in Scripture, when God did that, he said something. He stated a proposition, a sentence or sentences. He talked. But the idea now, which I think is mistaken, is that he kind of hints, nudges, drops little suggestions of what he has in mind, and we have to take these little nudge, nudge, hint, hint, subjective things and cobble them together. And we call that variously finding God's will, if the question is what should I do next in my life, or how do I make this particular decision, we might say, well, I'm learning to hear the voice of God, and we feel like there's something coming through, a sort of thing. And then, or something like that. So these are ways that people talk about receiving messages of some sort, and they might characterize it as hearing, even though some might mean, well, hearing means actually, for all intents and purposes, having a conversational relationship with God. Some use that language. So the hearing the voice of God is a very, very broad kind of characterization of God communicating with us in some way, and underneath that, people would also use the phrase, well, being led by the Spirit. Now, this is especially understood to be a kind of a nudging or pushing in a certain way, ergo the language led. Although technically, I think if you're led, you're being pulled, you're not being pushed, but that's inconsequential. That's an inconsequential decision. It's that you're being instructed in some fashion, and generally this is a subjective element. You're feeling led, is the way people put it. And there may be some other objective elements, circumstances or whatever, that seem to verify the direction or what's the term that's often used? It's... It's when people bear witness, or there's some kind of other consequence that kind of fits together. It all seems to mesh together. And so all of this is part of this being led by the Spirit or, quote-unquote, hearing the voice of God.

Now, it turns out, the phrase led by the Spirit is actually a scriptural phrase. The word I was looking for a moment ago is confirmation. People feel led, and then something else objectively might happen that would confirm in their mind the feeling of being led. But led by the Spirit is a phrase that you actually find in the New Testament. Now, ironically, or maybe surprisingly to some of you, it only appears two times. A certain variation a third time—I'll talk about that in a moment—in the life of Jesus. But it only appears two times, led by the Spirit. And they are both Pauline. In other words, Paul is the one who's using the phrase. No one else uses that phrase. And he means the same thing in both cases by that phrase. And he doesn't mean feeling a nudge by the Holy Spirit to go in a certain direction, at least not in the way that most of us understand that sense. And one of the ways, one of the places you find it is in Romans 8. And Paul is talking about Christians now being regenerated and not living according to the flesh, but according to the Spirit. And those are two different ways of living. One is unregenerate, the other is regenerate. You are not in the flesh, Paul says, if the Spirit of God dwells in you. And if he doesn't, you're not his. Okay, so this is the thing that's being set up. But if, he says, since we are Christians and we have the Spirit, we're under obligation not to the flesh. I'm reading now verse 12, Romans 8. We are under obligation not to the flesh to live according to the flesh. That's how everybody else is doing that. If you're living according to the flesh, you must die. But if by the Spirit—now watch the language here—you are putting to death the deeds of the body, the flesh, you will live. For, verse 14, all who are being led by the Spirit of God, these, these, these are sons of God. These are sons of God. Wait, what do you mean led by the Spirit? Verse 14, what he said in verse 13, you're putting to death the deeds of the body. You are not living according to the flesh. That's deadly. You're living according to the Spirit, which is putting to death the deeds of the body. So being led by the Spirit is putting to death the deeds of the body. That's the parallelism. There is no hint in this passage at all that people are getting nudges by God to go in a certain direction or make certain decisions. It's not there. He's talking about something else. So being led by the Spirit, at least in Romans 8, is overcoming the fleshly desires by the power of the Spirit so that you live a more holy life, which is what God has for us. And there's more talk about that in Romans 8. Okay, well, that's the first place it's used. Here's the other place that it's used, Galatians chapter 5. It's curious that this chapter, at the end of the chapter, is talking about the battle between the flesh and the Spirit. Same topic. Verse 16, I say, walk by the Spirit and you will not carry out the desire of the flesh. Now the phrase walk by the Spirit is being used there. But it's in parallel. That's verse 16. Verse 17 talks about the flesh sets its desire against the Spirit and the Spirit against the flesh, for these are in opposition to one another so that you may not do the things that you please. But if you are led by the Spirit, there is our phrase again, you are not under the law. Why not? Because you're not fulfilling the deeds of the flesh in the Spirit. You're living out the holy thing that the law requires. And then he goes into deeds of the flesh in verse 20 and actually 19 through 21, and then he has the fruits of the Spirit that follows verse 22 and 23. So walk by the Spirit in verse 16 is parallel with led by the Spirit in verse 18. It all has to do with overcoming the flesh. That's the whole context. So in other words, led by the Spirit in Galatians 5, where Paul writes, means exactly the same thing as led by the Spirit in Romans 8, where Paul writes, overcoming the flesh by the power of the Spirit in this battle of the flesh and the Spirit. There is no intimation here either that this is a nudge, nudge, hint, hint thing. There is no sense in the biblical writings here in the New Testament that led by the Spirit is a means of making decisions other than, I guess you could say, when you're convicted of sin, you don't—you stop doing it and you do what's right. That's what Paul means. Now, you have another in what gospel of Matthew, maybe Luke, where Jesus is led by the Spirit into the wilderness and to be tempted by the devil. But it's interesting, the words that are used there. It's a very different characterization because the word led by the Spirit actually means, I don't have it right in front of me, but it means that the Spirit is taking Jesus out there. He's taken out. And it's the very same construction that you see where the text says that the devil took Jesus to the top of the mountain and he was able to view all of the kingdoms of the world. And the same language there. So led by the Spirit in Galatians chapter 5 and in Romans 8, the Paulineus, is a very different sense of the word than you find where Jesus is so-called led by the Spirit. It may even be a different language in some different translations, but the word means taken, taken by the Spirit, and it's a whole different kind of thing. So what this amounts to is that when we look at the words led by the Spirit in Scripture, there is no reason to take those words to mean what almost every single Christian means when they use that language from the Scripture. Of course, we're led by the Spirit. There it is, right there in the text. Yes, but what you mean, led by the Spirit, is very different than what Paul means led by the Spirit. He means something different. And when it comes to hearing the voice of God, well, that's a broader category of which this led by the Spirit might be considered a subset. And the real question is, does God whisper? Do we have to learn to hear the voice of God? And the answer to that, simply, biblically, is no, because there's no place anywhere in Scripture where God spoke to someone and they didn't hear Him. He doesn't try. He's not a wannabe. He's a B.
